question: What do I need to do to qualify for the certificate if I’m starting late?
answer_llm:
If you’re starting late, you can still get the certificate, but you need to:

1. **Submit your Capstone project while submissions are still open**
2. **Pass the Capstone project**
3. **Participate in the peer-review of three peers in your cohort**

Homework is not mandatory for the certificate.

question: Where should I begin if I’m just starting LLM Zoomcamp, and what pages or repo should I look at first?
answer_llm:
If you’re just starting LLM Zoomcamp, begin with these:

1. The **LLM Zoomcamp docs**:  
   https://datatalks.club/docs/courses/llm-zoomcamp/

2. The **general Zoomcamp logistics docs**:  
   https://datatalks.club/docs/courses/zoomcamp-logistics/

3. The **LLM Zoomcamp GitHub repository**:  
   https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp

The repo is the main entry point. Open the lesson folders there, and use the lesson pages to find the relevant videos at the top. If you’re unsure where to go next, follow the GitHub repo first since it’s easier to keep updated than the YouTube playlist.
answer_orig:
Start with the [LLM Zoomcamp docs](https://datatalks.club/docs/courses/llm-zoomcamp/), the [general Zoomcamp logistics docs](https://datatalks.club/docs/courses/zoomcamp-logistics/), and the [LLM Zoomcamp GitHub repository](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp).

You can start whenever you want. The videos and GitHub materials are available, and the deadlines are listed in the [course management platform](https://courses.datatalks.club/llm-zoomcamp-2026/).

A typical workflow is:

1. Watch the lesson videos.
2. Work through the lesson notebooks/code.
3. Read the homework instructions on GitHub.
4. Submit answers through the course platform before the deadline.

Homework is similar to the lesson flow, but uses a different dataset or slightly different task.

question: How can I find my own entry on the leaderboard if I’m not sure which one is mine?
answer_llm: Click the **“Jump to your record on the leaderboard”** link. Your account is assigned a random display name automatically, so that link will take you directly to your entry. If you want to see or change the name shown publicly, use **“Edit Course Profile.”**
answer_orig:
When you set up your account, you are automatically assigned a random name, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.” Click on the "Jump to your record on the leaderboard" link to find your entry.

If you want to see what your Display name is, click on the "Edit Course Profile" button.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **First field:** This is your nickname/displayed name. You can change it if you want to be known by your Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname of your choice. This is useful if you want to remain anonymous.
- **Second field:** Change this to your official name as in your identification documents—passport, national ID card, driver's license, etc. This is mandatory if you do not want "Lucid Elbakyan" on your certificate. This name will appear on your Certificate!

question: Why do I see a random name on the leaderboard, and can I change it?
answer_llm:
You see a random name because the system automatically assigns a random display name when your account is created, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.”

Yes, you can change it:
- Open **Edit Course Profile**
- Change the **first field** to the nickname/display name you want shown on the leaderboard
- The **second field** is for your official name, which appears on your certificate

So the public leaderboard name is separate from your certificate name.

question: Can I take the course on my own schedule and still get the certificate, or is it only for the live cohort?
answer_llm: You can only get the certificate if you finish the course with the live cohort. The self-paced mode does **not** qualify for a certificate because you need to peer-review capstone projects while the course is running.

No, you can only get a certificate if you finish the course with a "live" cohort.

question: Why does the homework content keep changing before it’s released?
answer_llm:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means the homework is not ready yet, so the content can still change before release.

The homework is only ready when:
- the homework form is open on the course management platform, and
- the homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, changes are expected, so working on it early is at your own risk.
answer_orig:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means the homework is not ready yet.

The homework is ready only when both are true:

- The homework form is open on the course management platform.
- The homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change. Working on the material or homework in advance is at your own risk, because the final version can be different.

question: Why don't we use LangChain in this course, and what do we learn first instead?
answer_llm:
We don’t use LangChain in this course because we want to learn the core building blocks ourselves first: prompting, retrieval, indexing, and evaluation.

So, before using a framework, we first learn the fundamentals—similar to learning HTML, CSS, and JavaScript before React or Angular.
answer_orig:
LangChain is a framework for building LLM-powered apps. In this course, we first build the core pieces ourselves: prompting, retrieval, indexing, and evaluation.

question: Why do I get an OpenAI API error about insufficient credits when I run responses.create?
answer_llm:
Because your OpenAI API account doesn’t have enough credits or quota. The course notes say `responses.create` can return:

`OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits`

There is no free usage of OpenAI’s API, so you need to:

1. Add billing credits to your account.
2. Make sure you’re using a model available to your account.
3. If needed, recreate your API key after adding credits.

If you still see a 429-style quota error, it means the same billing/quota issue.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: I’m seeing an OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits on responses.create — is this a billing issue?
answer_llm:
Yes — **`OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits` is a billing/quota issue**.

It means your OpenAI API account doesn’t have enough credits or quota to run `responses.create`. The fix is to:

1. **Add credits / enable pay-as-you-go** in your OpenAI billing settings.
2. **Make sure you’re using a model available to your account**.
3. If needed, **recreate your API key** after adding credits and update it locally.

If you’re using **OpenRouter** instead of OpenAI directly, a similar error can also happen if `max_output_tokens` is too high, but for **OpenAI’s `responses.create`**, this error is related to **billing/quota**.

question: Why am I getting a 429 RateLimitError from OpenAI even though my code looks fine?
answer_llm:
You’re getting a 429 because it’s often not a code bug at all — it’s your OpenAI account quota/billing.

The context says this error usually means:

- **You exceeded your current quota**
- OpenAI returned **`insufficient_quota`**
- There is **no free usage** of the OpenAI API

What to do:

1. **Add billing credits** to your OpenAI account
   - Minimum suggested: **$5**
2. **Make sure you’re using a model available to your account**
3. **Regenerate your API key** after adding credits, then update it locally

If you’re just hitting rate limits during many requests, also reduce API calls and add retries:

```python
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
```

If you want, I can also help you tell the difference between a true rate limit and a quota/billing issue.

question: Why does the course use uv instead of pip and virtualenv for Python setup?
answer_llm:
The course uses **uv** because it’s **fast and convenient**. It’s an **all-in-one tool** that can replace tools like **pip, virtualenv, pip-tools, pipx, poetry, pyenv, and twine**, so you don’t have to juggle multiple tools.

It also helps with:
- managing **Python versions**
- creating **virtual environments**
- handling **dependencies**
- producing **reproducible lockfiles**

You **don’t have to use it**—**pip and virtualenv still work fine**—but `uv` makes the setup **simpler and faster**, which is why the lessons use it.

You're not required to use it — pip and virtualenv still work fine — but uv makes the setup simpler and faster, which is why the lessons use it.

Official docs: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/

question: What makes uv better or easier than using separate tools like pip, virtualenv, and pip-tools?
answer_llm:
`uv` is better or easier because it’s an all-in-one, fast tool that replaces several separate ones:

- `pip` for installing packages
- `virtualenv` for creating environments
- `pip-tools` for lockfiles
- plus others like `pipx`, `poetry`, `pyenv`, and `twine`

So instead of juggling multiple tools, you manage:

- Python versions
- virtual environments
- dependencies
- reproducible lockfiles

all through one command. The course uses it because it makes setup simpler and faster.

You don’t have to use it, though — `pip` and `virtualenv` still work fine.

question: Why does my Jupyter notebook say OPENAI_API_KEY is missing even though I set it in .env?

Your notebook is probably not loading the `.env` file automatically.

In Jupyter, you need to install and use `python-dotenv`, then load the file before creating the client:

```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()
```

Also make sure the variable name in `.env` is exactly:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
```

If it still fails, check that the `.env` file is in the project root.

question: How do I keep my API keys in a .env file and read them in Python?
answer_llm:
Store your keys in a `.env` file and read them with `python-dotenv`.

1. Add `.env` to `.gitignore`:
```gitignore
.env
```

2. Create a `.env` file:
```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_...
GEMINI_API_KEY=...
```

3. Install `python-dotenv`:
```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

4. Load the file in Python:
```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

openai_api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
groq_api_key = os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY")
```

Make sure the variable name in `.env` matches exactly, like `OPENAI_API_KEY`.

question: How can I plug a Google Gemini model into the Module 1 notebook using the OpenAI-style endpoint?
answer_llm:
You can plug Gemini into the Module 1 notebook by using the OpenAI SDK with Google’s OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

Use:

- your Gemini API key from `.env`
- `base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"`
- a Gemini model name, such as `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`

Example:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

def llm(instructions, user_prompt, model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite"):
    message_history = [
        {"role": "developer", "content": instructions},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}
    ]

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=message_history
    )

    return response.choices[0].message.content
```

This uses the older chat-completions style through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so you may need to adjust notebook code that assumes the newer Responses API, especially around reading outputs and handling tools/function calls.

question: In the Module 1 examples, what changes when I use the older chat completions API instead of the newer Responses format?
answer_llm:
When you use the older `chat.completions` API instead of the newer Responses format, a few things change:

- **How you read the answer**
  - Responses API: `response.output`
  - Chat completions: `response.choices[0].message.content`

- **How you call the model**
  - Responses API uses `client.responses.create(...)`
  - Chat completions uses `client.chat.completions.create(...)`

- **Tool/function handling**
  - You may need to **adapt the tools schema** to match the provider’s expected shape.

In the Module 1 examples, the main practical change is that you replace the newer Responses-style response handling with chat-completions-style handling, and you may need to adjust tools/function calls accordingly.

question: How do I get Gemini to return Pydantic objects through the OpenAI-compatible API if `responses.parse` doesn’t work?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI SDK’s **chat completions parsing flow** instead of `responses.parse`.

Define your Pydantic models, then call:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)
```

Then read the parsed object from:

```python
result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
```

Example:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]
```

This works with Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint and gives you Pydantic objects directly, without manual JSON parsing.
answer_orig:
To get parsed structured output, use the OpenAI SDK's chat-completions parsing flow instead of the newer Responses API. This is the right choice when you want to stay on the OpenAI SDK but call a chat-completions-compatible model like Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

First, define the structure you want Gemini to return as a Pydantic model:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]
```

Then call `chat.completions.parse` with `response_format=Questions`. Pass the same kind of `messages` you would to any chat completion:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

Notes:
- This approach keeps using the OpenAI SDK while leveraging Gemini through Google's endpoint.
- The parsed output (e.g., `result.questions`) is a Pydantic object available directly, with no manual JSON parsing.
- `chat.completions.parse` requires `pydantic` (`uv add pydantic`).

```python
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

Notes:
- This approach keeps using the OpenAI SDK while leveraging Gemini through Google's endpoint.
- The parsed output (e.g., `result.questions`) is a Pydantic object available directly, with no manual JSON parsing.
- `chat.completions.parse` requires `pydantic` (`uv add pydantic`).

- pass `response_format=YourPydanticModel`
- access the parsed object with `response.choices[0].message.parsed`

question: How do I install Ollama on Mac, Windows, or Linux?
answer_llm:
To install Ollama, go to https://ollama.com/download and choose your operating system:

- macOS: download the `.pkg` file and install it
- Windows: download the `.msi` file and install it
- Linux: run

```bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
```

After installation, you can test it by running:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```
answer_orig:
First, install Ollama by visiting [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download) and choosing your operating system:

- **macOS**: Download the `.pkg` and install it.
- **Windows**: Download the `.msi` and install it.
- **Linux**: Run the following command in the terminal:

  ```bash
  curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
  ```

Once installed, open a terminal and type:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

This command will:

- Download the LLaMA 3 model (~4GB).
- Start the model locally.
- Open a chat-like interface where you can type questions.

To test the Ollama local server, run the following command:

```bash
curl http://localhost:11434
```

You should receive a response similar to:

```json
{"models": [...]}  
```

Then, install the Python client with:

```bash
pip install ollama
```

Here is a minimal Python example:

```python
import ollama

response = ollama.chat(
    model='llama3',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": your_prompt}]
)

print(response['message']['content'])
```

question: Why does tiktoken show a smaller token count than OpenAI’s chat API for the same prompt?
answer_llm:
Because `tiktoken.encode()` only counts the tokens in your visible text, while OpenAI’s chat API also adds internal formatting tokens for each message.

In particular:

- each chat message adds about 4 structural tokens
- the API adds 2 extra tokens to start assistant generation
- extra whitespace, newlines, or unusual Unicode can also change the count

So `tiktoken` can show a smaller number even for the same prompt.
answer_orig:
When using `tiktoken.encode()` to count tokens in your prompt, you might see a difference compared to OpenAI’s API response. For instance, you might get 320 tokens, while OpenAI reports 327. This is due to internal tokens added by OpenAI’s chat formatting.

Here’s what happens:

- Each message in a `chat.completions.create()` call (e.g., `{role: "user", content: "..."}`) adds 4 structural tokens (role, content, separators).
- The API adds 2 tokens globally to mark the start of assistant response generation.
- Extra newlines, whitespace, or uncommon Unicode characters in your content may slightly increase the token count.

Thus, even if your visible text is 320 tokens, OpenAI may count 327 due to these internal additions.

question: How come my prompt is 320 tokens locally but the API says 327 tokens?

Because OpenAI counts a few **extra internal chat-formatting tokens** that your local `tiktoken.encode()` count doesn’t include.

Typically:

- **Each chat message** adds about **4 structural tokens**
- The API adds about **2 tokens** to signal the assistant response start
- Small things like **newlines, whitespace, or Unicode characters** can add a bit more

So a visible prompt that looks like **320 tokens locally** can show up as **327 tokens** in the API due to those hidden formatting tokens.

question: How can I set my API key once so I don’t have to export it in every new terminal?
answer_llm:
Set it in your shell startup file so it’s loaded automatically in new terminals.

For example, add this to `~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc`:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
```

Then reload the file:

```bash
source ~/.bashrc
# or
source ~/.zshrc
```

If you also need Kestra flows to see it, you’ll still need the base64-encoded `SECRET_` version before starting Kestra:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)
```

Then restart Kestra:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```
answer_orig:
Use [`dirdotenv`](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/dirdotenv). It is like `direnv`, but works with both `.env` and `.envrc`, and is more portable across shells and operating systems.

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add this line to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh

# inside your project:
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project directory.

Important: always add `.env` and `.envrc` to `.gitignore` so keys never land on GitHub.

`direnv` is also fine if you already use it. In that case, create `.envrc`, add your exports there, and run `direnv allow`.

For GitHub Codespaces, use the built-in [Codespaces secrets](https://docs.github.com/en/codespaces/managing-your-codespaces/managing-your-account-specific-secrets-for-github-codespaces) instead of files in the repo.

For Python scripts, the equivalent is `python-dotenv`:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # loads .env from project root
```

Example:



question: How do I decide the best field weights for minsearch or a similar search engine?
answer_llm:
The best way is to **test different field weights against a ground-truth set**.

A good process is:

1. Create a small set of representative questions.
2. Mark which documents should be retrieved for each question.
3. Try different field weight combinations.
4. Compare results using metrics like **hit rate, precision@k, recall@k, or MRR**.

For small projects, you can also tune weights by **trial and error**, but evaluation on a labeled dataset is the more reliable approach.

question: What’s the best way to clean documents before using them in RAG?
answer_llm:
Clean documents by making them **clean, structured, and easy to chunk/retrieve**.

Practical steps:
- Remove obvious noise: broken HTML, duplicate text, boilerplate, OCR errors, repeated headers/footers.
- Preserve useful context: titles, section names, dates, page numbers, speaker names, Q&A structure.
- Store the text in a structured format if helpful, such as JSON.
- Chunk the text so related context stays together.
- Keep useful metadata for later filtering or ranking.

The goal is not just to shorten the text, but to make retrieval more accurate.

question: In GitHub Codespaces, when uv prints 'Failed to hardlink files', should I worry or is it safe to ignore?
answer_llm:
No, you usually don’t need to worry. In GitHub Codespaces, `uv` may print **"Failed to hardlink files"** when it can’t hardlink between the cache and the target environment, but the install still succeeds.

`uv` will just **fall back to copying files**.

If you want to hide the warning for the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```
answer_orig:
No. This warning can happen in GitHub Codespaces when `uv` cannot hardlink files between the cache and the target environment.

The package still installs. `uv` falls back to copying files.

To suppress the warning for the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

See the `uv` documentation for more details: [https://docs.astral.sh/uv/](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/).

question: Why does WSL2 say the model needs more system memory when my PC actually has enough RAM installed?
answer_llm:
WSL2 only sees the memory allocated to the WSL virtual machine, not all the RAM installed in your PC. So even if your computer has enough total RAM, WSL2 may still report that less is available to it.

To give WSL2 more memory, set it in `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`, for example:

```ini
[wsl2]
memory=8GB
```

Then restart WSL:

```bash
wsl --shutdown
```

You can verify it inside WSL with `free`.
answer_orig:
Your WSL2 is set to use Y.Y GiB, not all your computer memory. To allocate more RAM, follow these steps:

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

For more details, read [this article](https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/limiting-memory-usage-in-wsl-2/).

question: How do I increase the RAM available to WSL2 so a large model can run without the ResponseError?
answer_llm:
Your WSL2 is probably limited to less RAM than your Windows machine has.

To give WSL2 more memory:

1. Create a file named `.wslconfig` in your Windows user profile directory, for example:
   `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`

2. Put this in the file:
   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL:
   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Check the available RAM inside WSL with:
   ```bash
   free
   ```

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini

   ```

   ```bash

   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

question: Why does my GitHub Codespace get stuck on "Finishing up" and what should I try first?
answer_llm:
GitHub Codespaces reliability issues can cause it to get stuck on **“Finishing up”**. There isn’t one guaranteed fix, but **the first thing to try** is:

1. Go to **github.com/codespaces**
2. **Stop** the codespace
3. **Start it again**

If that doesn’t work, try opening it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or use a different browser like **Edge, Chrome, or Brave**.
answer_orig:
These are usually GitHub Codespaces reliability issues rather than a problem with the course, so there's no single guaranteed fix — but the following workarounds resolve most cases.

**Codespace won't connect / stuck on "Finishing up":**

- Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces), stop the codespace, and start it again.
- If it still won't connect, open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try a different browser (Edge/Chrome/Brave).
- As a last resort, delete the codespace and create a new one.

**"My setup is all gone":**

- The repo in `/workspaces` persists across stop/start, but a **rebuild or a brand-new codespace** starts from a clean image, and system/global installs outside your project don't always survive. Reinstalling is quick with `uv` (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`).
- **Commit and push your work often** — uncommitted changes survive a stop/start but are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace.

If Codespaces keeps being flaky for you, consider running the course locally instead — see "[Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?](026_aa310de435_can-i-run-the-course-locally-instead-of-codespaces.md)".

question: If Codespaces won’t reconnect, is there a browser workaround before I delete it?
answer_llm:
Yes — if it still won’t connect, try opening the Codespace in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try a different browser like **Edge, Chrome, or Brave** before deleting it.

If that still doesn’t work, then delete the Codespace and create a new one.

question: Can I swap OpenAI for another LLM provider in the RAG module, or am I stuck with OpenAI only?
answer_llm:
No, you’re not stuck with OpenAI only.

In the RAG module, you can use other LLM providers such as Gemini, Groq, OpenRouter, Azure OpenAI, local models, or any provider with an OpenAI-compatible endpoint. You may need to adjust things like the `base_url`, API key, model name, and sometimes response/tool formats depending on the provider.
answer_orig:
If the provider used in the course isn't available or is blocked in your region (or you simply prefer another one), you can use any other LLM provider — the course isn't tied to OpenAI. Just switch to something else:

- Hosted, OpenAI-compatible providers — e.g. Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral. The course code uses the OpenAI client, so you usually only need to change the `base_url`, the API key, and the model name.
- Open models via Hugging Face (e.g. Qwen, Llama) if you prefer hosted open-source models.
- Serve a model locally with [Ollama](https://ollama.com/), [vLLM](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm), LM Studio, or anything else — no external API call at all, so regional blocks don't apply and you don't need a paid key. Most of these also expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so the course code works with only a `base_url` change.
- Rent a GPU machine and serve the model there (e.g. with vLLM) if your own machine can't run the model you want. This gives you a private OpenAI-compatible endpoint to point the course code at — just remember to stop/delete the instance when you're done so you don't keep paying for it.
- A VPN also works if you just need to reach a provider that's blocked at the network level.

Anything with an OpenAI-compatible endpoint (or a locally served model) will work — pick whatever is available and convenient for you.

For a curated list of options, see [Awesome LLMs](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo, which collects OpenAI API alternatives and tools for running models locally.

question: Why do I keep getting 429 errors from OpenAI when I run the notebook over lots of docs, and what should I do first?
answer_llm:
You’re probably hitting OpenAI’s per-minute or per-day rate limits because the notebook makes lots of API calls in a loop over many documents. Free-tier and low-spend accounts can exhaust those limits quickly.

What to do first:
1. **Reduce the number of API calls** before dealing with retries.
2. **Cache results** so re-running the notebook doesn’t re-call the API for the same inputs.
3. **Lower concurrency** if you’re using a worker pool.
4. **Batch requests** where possible.

Also, if the error is actually `insufficient_quota`, that’s not a rate limit problem — it means your account billing/quota is exhausted, and you need to add funds.
answer_orig:
Free-tier and low-spend OpenAI accounts have per-minute and per-day request limits that are easy to blow through when a notebook loops over many documents. Reduce the number of API calls before you worry about handling the error after the fact.

- **Retry with backoff.** The `openai` Python client retries automatically with exponential backoff; raise the limit so transient 429s resolve themselves instead of crashing the run:

  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI

  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```

- **Lower concurrency.** In a thread/process pool, keep the pool small (2-3 workers) so you stay under the per-minute cap. A larger pool finishes one batch fast and then fails on the next.
- **Cache results.** Write embeddings and LLM responses to disk (JSONL, pickle, or a vector DB) and reload on re-run. Re-executing a notebook should not re-call the API for inputs you already processed.
- **Batch where possible.** Group independent inputs into a single request (e.g. embed a list of texts in one call) instead of looping one-by-one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider.** `gpt-4o-mini` is cheap enough for the whole course; Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) has generous per-minute limits via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

If you already see `insufficient_quota`, that is a billing issue, not a rate limit — see [OpenAI: Error: RateLimitError: Error code: 429](openai-error-ratelimiterror-error-code-429).

question: How can I make my OpenAI calls retry automatically instead of failing on the first rate-limit hit?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI client’s built-in retries with exponential backoff:

```python
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
```

That way, transient 429 rate-limit errors are retried automatically instead of failing on the first hit.

question: Should I save embeddings and LLM outputs somewhere so rerunning the notebook doesn’t hit the API again?
answer_llm: Yes — cache results. Save embeddings and LLM responses to disk, such as in JSONL, pickle, or a vector DB, and reload them on re-run so the notebook doesn’t call the API again for inputs you’ve already processed.
answer_orig:
Free-tier and low-spend OpenAI accounts have per-minute and per-day request limits that are easy to blow through when a notebook loops over many documents. Reduce the number of API calls before you worry about handling the error after the fact.

- **Retry with backoff.** The `openai` Python client retries automatically with exponential backoff; raise the limit so transient 429s resolve themselves instead of crashing the run:

  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI

  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```

- **Lower concurrency.** In a thread/process pool, keep the pool small (2-3 workers) so you stay under the per-minute cap. A larger pool finishes one batch fast and then fails on the next.
- **Cache results.** Write embeddings and LLM responses to disk (JSONL, pickle, or a vector DB) and reload on re-run. Re-executing a notebook should not re-call the API for inputs you already processed.
- **Batch where possible.** Group independent inputs into a single request (e.g. embed a list of texts in one call) instead of looping one-by-one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider.** `gpt-4o-mini` is cheap enough for the whole course; Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) has generous per-minute limits via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

If you already see `insufficient_quota`, that is a billing issue, not a rate limit — see [OpenAI: Error: RateLimitError: Error code: 429](openai-error-ratelimiterror-error-code-429).

question: In agentic RAG, what counts as a tool or function, and how is it different from the normal RAG setup?
answer_llm:
In agentic RAG, a **tool or function** is anything the model can call for external help, for example:

- searching documents
- querying a database
- calling an API
- running a calculation

The key difference from normal RAG is:

- **Agentic RAG:** the model **decides when** to use a tool/function.
- **Basic/normal RAG:** retrieval is usually a **fixed step** that happens **before** the model answers.

So the main shift is from a pre-set retrieval pipeline to a model that can choose actions dynamically.
answer_orig:
In the context of this course, tools and functions are closely related terms. Do not worry too much about the naming difference.

A tool/function is something the model can call when it needs external help, such as searching documents, querying a database, calling an API, or running a calculation.

The important idea is that in agentic RAG, the model can decide when to call a tool/function. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a fixed step that happens before the model answers.

question: Why does agentic RAG need the model to decide when to use a tool instead of always retrieving first?
answer_llm:
Because in agentic RAG the model should **choose whether retrieval is actually needed**.

If you always retrieve first, you may:
- do unnecessary work for questions the model can answer directly,
- add latency and cost,
- retrieve irrelevant context that can hurt the answer.

Agentic RAG lets the model call a tool only when it needs external help, which makes the system more flexible than basic RAG, where retrieval is a fixed step before answering.

question: Why am I getting `AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'` when I call the Responses API on a model that isn’t from OpenAI?
answer_llm:
Because `client.responses.create(...)` and `response.output` are part of OpenAI’s newer Responses API, and that API is not implemented by most non-OpenAI providers like Mistral, Groq, or Gemini.

So when you call it against a non-OpenAI model, the returned object isn’t a Responses API object, which leads to:

```python
AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'
```

For non-OpenAI providers, use the chat-completions API instead and read:

```python
response.choices[0].message.content
```

Example:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You may also need to adapt the tools schema to the provider’s expected format.
answer_orig:
The new OpenAI Responses API (`client.responses.create(...)`, accessed via `response.output`) is OpenAI-specific. Other providers (Mistral, Groq, Gemini, etc.) don't implement it.

For non-OpenAI providers, use the chat-completions API and read `response.choices[0].message.content`:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # may need adapting per provider
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You'll also have to adapt the tools schema to whatever shape your provider expects.

It is okay to use the older `chat.completions` API for homework or projects if your provider supports that interface better than the Responses API.

question: Can I use `client.responses.create(...)` with Mistral, Groq, Gemini, or other non-OpenAI models, or does that only work with OpenAI models?
answer_llm:
`client.responses.create(...)` is **OpenAI-specific**. It works with OpenAI’s Responses API, but **not** with Mistral, Groq, Gemini, or most other non-OpenAI providers.

For those providers, use the **chat completions** API instead, for example:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # may need adapting per provider
)

content = response.choices[0].message.content
```

So in short:

- **OpenAI models**: `client.responses.create(...)` can be used
- **Non-OpenAI providers**: usually **use `chat.completions.create(...)`**
- You may also need to **adapt tool schemas and response parsing** depending on the provider

question: Why does toyaikit give me a 404 on Groq or another non-OpenAI API, and what should I change so it works?
answer_llm:
Because `toyaikit`’s `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` use OpenAI’s **Responses API** (`responses.create`), and Groq or other non-OpenAI providers don’t implement that endpoint. That’s why you get a `404`.

What to change:
- **Don’t use** `OpenAIResponsesRunner`
- **Do use** the **chat completions** version instead:
  - `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient`
  - `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner`

For Groq, point the OpenAI client at Groq’s base URL:

```python
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)

llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(
    model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile",
    client=groq_client,
)
```

For Anthropic, use:
- `AnthropicClient`
- `AnthropicMessagesRunner`

So the key fix is: **switch from Responses API classes to chat-completions / messages classes that your provider supports.**
answer_orig:
If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

### Anthropic

```python
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import AnthropicClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import AnthropicMessagesRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically:
llm_client = AnthropicClient(model="claude-haiku-4-5")

runner = AnthropicMessagesRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

Notes:

- `runner.loop(prompt=...)` runs one turn programmatically and returns a result with `.last_message`, `.all_messages`, and `.tokens` — no chat interface needed. Use `runner.run()` only for the interactive Jupyter loop.
- Pass an explicit, current model id. For Anthropic, `claude-haiku-4-5` / `claude-sonnet-4-5` work; an outdated id can 404.
- For Groq you'll see a harmless `UnknownModelWarning: No pricing data...` — the call still succeeds, only cost calculation is skipped.

question: Why do I get a 402 from OpenRouter when I call responses.create(), and how can I fix it?
answer_llm:
OpenRouter can return a `402` from `responses.create()` when you don’t set a reasonable `max_output_tokens`. OpenRouter checks billing/limits against the **maximum possible output**, which can be very large, so a free or low-limit key may be rejected before the model runs.

### How to fix it
Set a lower `max_output_tokens` value, for example:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

If you’re using the Module 1 `rag_helper.py`, add the same parameter in the `llm()` method.

For ToyAIKit:

```python
llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

### Also check
- Your `.env` should point to OpenRouter:
  - `OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...`
  - `OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1`
  - `OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`
- Check your OpenRouter key limits/credits.
- Prefer a pinned model like `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free` instead of `openrouter/free`.

If you still have issues after that, I don't know.
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

question: In the Module 1 homework, when people say to point RAG at the chunk index, does that mean I should index the split docs instead of the full ones?
answer_llm:
Yes — it means you should index the split documents/chunks, not the full original documents.

So in Module 1, “point RAG at the chunk index” means something like:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

not:

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

The idea is that retrieval happens over smaller chunks, and then only the relevant chunks are passed into the prompt.

Then the RAG pipeline retrieves relevant chunks and puts only those chunks into the prompt. This is what reduces the amount of context sent to the model.

question: Why does the RAG pipeline use chunks in the prompt instead of sending the whole document text?
answer_llm:
Because the chunked documents are what the retrieval step finds most relevant.

Using chunks in the prompt:

- reduces the amount of context sent to the model
- avoids sending unnecessary text from the whole document
- makes retrieval more focused on the specific passage needed to answer the question

So in RAG, you build/search the index on `chunks`, retrieve the best matching chunks, and only include those chunks in the prompt rather than the entire document.

question: How should I count tokens for the Module 1 homework if I'm using a different LLM provider?
answer_llm:
For Module 1 homework, count tokens from the **model response object** if your provider exposes usage info.

- OpenAI-compatible clients often return fields like `response.usage.input_tokens` or `response.usage.prompt_tokens`.
- If you use a non-OpenAI provider, check that provider’s **native usage fields** and adapt your code.
- **Do not** use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, or other providers, because tokenization differs.
- If the provider does not expose token usage, use that provider’s **native tokenizer** as an approximation.

If you need a multiple-choice answer, choose the **closest option**.

question: Why does the homework checker say my repo link is not returning 200, and how can I make it pass?
answer_llm:
The checker does a GET request to your submitted URL and expects an HTTP `200` response.

If it’s not 200, the most common fixes are:

- Make the repo **public**
- Submit the **plain repo URL**, not one ending in `.git`
  - Good: `https://github.com/you/repo`
  - Bad: `https://github.com/you/repo.git`
- Double-check for a **typo** in the link

A good test is to open the URL in an **incognito/private browser window**. If it loads there, it should pass the checker too.
answer_orig:
The submission checker fetches the URL you submit with a GET request and expects an HTTP `200` response. A non-200 status (404, 500, etc.) almost always means the link isn't publicly reachable as-is. Check the usual causes:

- **Private repo** — make the repository public so the checker can access it.
- **Trailing `.git`** — submit the plain repository URL (e.g. `https://github.com/you/repo`), not `https://github.com/you/repo.git`.
- **Typo in the URL** — paste the exact link.

A quick test: open the URL in a private/incognito browser window. If it loads for you there, it'll work for the checker too.

- **Trailing `.git`** — submit the plain repository URL (e.g. `https://github.com/you/repo`), not `https://github.com/you/repo.git`.
- **Typo in the URL** — paste the exact link.

A quick test: open the URL in a private/incognito browser window. If it loads for you there, it'll work for the checker too.

question: What exactly are embeddings in vector search, and why do we turn non-numerical data into numbers?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context.

Why turn text or other non-numerical data into numbers?

- So we can use mathematical algorithms on it
- So similar items end up with similar numeric representations
- So we can measure semantic similarity using vector math

In vector search, this means that texts with similar meanings are mapped to nearby points in a vector space. That proximity lets us search by meaning instead of exact words.

Related ideas: vector space model, dimensionality reduction.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: How do embeddings keep the meaning and context of text when converting it into numerical form?
answer_llm: Embeddings convert text into numbers in a way that preserves meaning and context by placing similar pieces of text close together in a vector space. So texts with related meanings get similar numerical representations, which lets mathematical similarity methods compare them effectively.

question: Why should similar pieces of input end up with similar vectors in an embedding model?
answer_llm: Because the embedding model is supposed to preserve meaning and context: if two inputs are similar, they should map to nearby points in vector space so their numerical vectors are close too. That way, similarity can be measured with things like cosine similarity.

question: What’s the connection between embeddings, the vector space model, and dimensionality reduction?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are a way to turn text or other non-numerical data into vectors of numbers while preserving meaning. That’s the same basic idea behind the **vector space model**: represent items as points in a vector space so that similar items end up close together.

**Dimensionality reduction** often comes in because many classic embedding methods start from very high-dimensional representations and then compress them into a smaller vector space while trying to keep the important semantic structure.

- **Vector space model**: the general framework of representing data as vectors.
- **Embeddings**: a concrete way to create those vectors from text/data.
- **Dimensionality reduction**: often the technique used to make those vectors lower-dimensional while preserving meaning.

question: Why did the lesson just use dot product for similarity, but the homework makes us normalize vectors first?
answer_llm:
In the lesson, `.dot(...)` was used directly because the embeddings were already normalized to unit length, so dot product = cosine similarity.

In the homework, the embeddings you use are **not normalized by default** (for example, TF-IDF + SVD), so you need to normalize them first; otherwise the dot product is not the same as cosine similarity.
answer_orig:
In the lesson, `.dot(...)` was used under the assumption that the embeddings returned by the model (e.g., `model.encode(...)` from OpenAI) are already normalized to have unit length. In that case, the dot product is mathematically equivalent to cosine similarity.

In the homework, however, we use classic embeddings like TF-IDF + SVD, which are not normalized by default. This means that the dot product does not represent cosine similarity unless we manually normalize the vectors.

question: Is dot product really the same as cosine similarity in the lecture example, and if so why?
answer_llm:
Yes — in the lecture example, dot product is effectively the same as cosine similarity **because the vectors are normalized to unit length**.

Why that works:
- Cosine similarity is `dot(a, b) / (||a|| * ||b||)`
- If both vectors already have length 1, then `||a|| = ||b|| = 1`
- So the formula becomes just `dot(a, b)`

That’s why the lesson can use `.dot(...)` directly.

In the homework, normalization is emphasized because the embeddings there are **not always normalized by default**. In that case, plain dot product is **not** the same as cosine similarity unless you normalize first.

So the short answer is:
- **Yes, in the lecture example**
- **Only because the embeddings are normalized**
- **Without normalization, dot product and cosine similarity are different**

question: When are dot product and cosine similarity equivalent, and when do they differ?
answer_llm:
Dot product and cosine similarity are equivalent when the vectors are **normalized to unit length**.

- If both vectors have norm 1, then:
  - **dot product = cosine similarity**
- This is why the lesson could use `.dot(...)` directly for normalized embeddings.

They differ when the vectors are **not normalized**.

- In that case, the dot product depends on both:
  - the angle between vectors, and
  - their magnitudes
- Cosine similarity ignores magnitude and measures only the angle.

So:
- **Equivalent:** normalized vectors
- **Different:** non-normalized vectors, like classic TF-IDF + SVD embeddings unless you normalize them first

question: For vector search, is it better to index just the answer text, just the question, or both together?
answer_llm:
There's no single right answer — it depends on your dataset and what gives the best hit rate / MRR on your ground-truth set.

The course suggests trying all three:
- index the answer text only
- index the question only
- index `question + " " + text`

Often `question + text` works best, but it’s a bit longer and slightly more expensive.
answer_orig:
There's no single right answer — it's an experiment to run on your dataset. The course shows three options:

- Embed the answer (`text`) only — works because the model captures semantic similarity between questions and their answers.
- Embed the question only — works because user queries look like the indexed questions.
- Embed `question + " " + text` — often the best, but produces longer input and slightly more cost.

Pick whichever gives the best hit rate / MRR on your ground-truth set. The course materials include a side-by-side comparison.

question: How does cosine similarity work when comparing two document vectors?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity measures the angle between two document vectors, not their length.

- If the vectors point in the same direction, similarity is close to 1.
- If they are unrelated and orthogonal, similarity is 0.
- If they point in opposite directions, similarity is -1.

For document comparison, the vectors are usually word counts or TF-IDF values. If the vectors are normalized to unit length, cosine similarity can be computed as a simple dot product between them.

If you'd like, I can also show the formula with a small numeric example.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity is a measure used to calculate the similarity between two non-zero vectors, often used in text analysis to determine how similar two documents are based on their content. This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: Why do people use cosine similarity in text analysis or vector search?
answer_llm:
People use cosine similarity because it measures how **similar the direction** of two vectors is, which works well for text. In text analysis, documents are represented as vectors of word counts, TF-IDF values, or embeddings, and cosine similarity tells us how close their content is **regardless of length or magnitude**.

In vector search, it is especially useful because if vectors are normalized, cosine similarity becomes just a **dot product**, which can be computed efficiently with matrix multiplication.

- **-1**: the vectors are **completely opposite**

- **0** = vectors are orthogonal, so there is no similarity

question: Why can cosine similarity be computed with a dot product once the vectors are normalized?
answer_llm:
Once vectors are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes are 1, so cosine similarity

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{u \cdot v}{\|u\|\|v\|}
\]

becomes

\[
\cos(\theta) = u \cdot v
\]

because \(\|u\| = \|v\| = 1\). So the cosine similarity is just the dot product.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: What changes when embeddings and the query are unit-length in vector search?
answer_llm: When both the embeddings and the query are unit-length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In that case, cosine similarity becomes just the dot product, so vector search can be done efficiently with matrix multiplication.

This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: How does normalizing vectors make cosine similarity easier to calculate efficiently?
answer_llm:
Normalizing vectors to unit length removes the magnitude from the calculation, so cosine similarity becomes just the dot product.

That makes it efficient because you can compute all similarities with a single matrix multiplication between the normalized document embeddings and the normalized query vector, instead of separately computing angles or dividing by vector norms each time.

question: Why is matrix multiplication enough to get cosine scores between a query and all documents?
answer_llm:
Because if the embeddings are **normalized to unit length**, cosine similarity becomes just the **dot product**.

So for one query vector and all document vectors:

- normalize the query
- normalize all document embeddings
- compute `X.dot(query)`

That matrix multiplication gives the cosine score for the query against every document in one efficient operation.

question: What does cosine similarity actually measure, and why does vector length stop mattering after normalization?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity measures **how aligned two vectors are**, meaning it focuses on the **angle between them**, not how long they are.

Why length stops mattering after normalization:
- Normalization scales each vector to **unit length**.
- Once vectors all have length 1, their magnitudes are the same, so only the **direction** remains relevant.
- In that case, cosine similarity becomes the **dot product**, which is why it can be computed efficiently with matrix multiplication.

So:
- **Before normalization:** dot product mixes together angle and vector length.
- **After normalization:** length is fixed, so the dot product measures only alignment, i.e. cosine similarity.

question: Why do the dot-product and loop versions of vector search give slightly different scores even though they should be the same?
answer_llm:
They differ only because of floating-point precision.

`X.dot(v_query)` and the loop compute the same dot products, but they sum the numbers in a different order. Since floating-point addition is not perfectly associative, you can get tiny differences in the last decimal places.

So the scores are effectively the same, just not bit-for-bit identical. Use a tolerance check like:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```
answer_orig:
In [Lesson 4 (Vector Search)](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/02-vector-search/lessons/04-vector-search.md) we compute the scores two ways — `scores = X.dot(v_query)` and the equivalent for-loop — and they can come out slightly different. This is normal, it's just floating-point precision. If you compare them with `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` you might get `False`, even though both compute the same dot products. They just add the numbers up in a slightly different order, and floating-point addition isn't perfectly associative, so the results can differ in the last few decimal places.

Under the hood, `X.dot(v)` runs optimized BLAS code that sums in a different order than a sequential Python loop. The math is the same; only the rounding differs, so the values aren't really "wrong."

Just compare them with a small tolerance instead of expecting an exact match:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

question: Why am I getting IndexError: list index out of range when I try to access the best chunk?
answer_llm:
The `IndexError: list index out of range` usually means your embeddings and chunks are misaligned.

Most likely, you generated a different number of embeddings than the number of chunks. When you later try to access the “best chunk,” the index refers to a position that doesn’t exist in the chunks list.

Make sure you create embeddings directly from the chunk list:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

Then `len(X)` should be equal to `len(chunks)`.

Also, if you’re using RAG, make sure you’re fitting/searching the index on the chunked documents, not the original full documents.
answer_orig:
The error typically happens when the number of embeddings you generate does not match the number of document chunks. Make sure you create embeddings directly from the chunk list:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

The number of rows in `X` should be equal to `len(chunks)`.

question: Why should the number of rows in the embedding matrix match len(chunks)?
answer_llm:
Because each row in the embedding matrix must correspond to one chunk, so you can look up the embedding for chunk *i* directly by using the same index. If the counts don’t match, you can get indexing errors like `IndexError: list index out of range` when retrieving the best chunk.

In practice, generate embeddings from the chunk contents:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

Then `X.shape[0]` should equal `len(chunks)`.

question: For Module 2 Q2, my cosine similarity doesn’t match any choice — is it because I used the wrong embedding model?
answer_llm:
Yes — that’s the most likely reason.

For Module 2 Homework Q2, you’re supposed to use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py`, not `sentence-transformers` or another embedding model:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

If you used a different model like `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` or `all-mpnet-base-v2`, your cosine similarity can end up not matching any option.

Also make sure you are:

- embedding the page’s `content` field
- comparing it to the query vector from Q1
- using normalized vectors

If you did all that and the value is still a little off, choose the closest option — small numerical differences can happen.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is using a different embedding model than the homework specifies. Homework 2 tells you **not** to use `sentence-transformers` and to use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py` instead:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

Both approaches produce the same vectors for the same model, but if you embed with a different model (for example the `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` model from the lessons, or `all-mpnet-base-v2`) you'll get a cosine value that isn't among the options.

Also check that:

- You're embedding the page's `content` field (not the filename or the whole dict).
- You're comparing against the query vector from Q1.
- The vectors are normalized — the `Embedder` returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity directly.

If your value is still slightly off after using the right model, pick the closest option — small numerical differences are expected.

question: How do I set up Gemini, OpenAI, and Tavily keys so Kestra can actually use them?
answer_llm:
Set the keys as environment variables **before starting Kestra**.

Use these:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # for AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # for Kestra flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # for web search flows
```

Then restart Kestra so it picks them up:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

In a flow, reference the secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

Important:
- **Copilot** uses the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- **Flows** use the base64-encoded `SECRET_...` variables
answer_orig:
Kestra reads secrets from environment variables that are prefixed with `SECRET_` and whose value is **base64-encoded**. You export them in the terminal *before* starting Kestra. For Gemini you need two variables — the plain one (used by the AI Copilot) and the base64-encoded `SECRET_` one (used by the flows):

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # used by AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # used by the flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for web search (flows 3, 5, 6)
```

Then start (or restart) Kestra so it picks up the variables:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

Inside a flow, reference a secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

See the [setup lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/03-setup.md) for the full walkthrough. Never commit your keys to Git.

question: Why does Kestra need both a normal Gemini API key and a SECRET_ one, and which one goes where?
answer_llm:
Kestra needs both because they are used by **different parts** of the system:

- **`GEMINI_API_KEY`** = the **plain** key for the **AI Copilot**
- **`SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`** = the **base64-encoded secret** for the **flows**

So you set both, like this:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)
```

Then:

- **Copilot** reads `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- **Flows** use `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}` and Kestra resolves that from `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`

In short: **plain key goes to the Copilot, SECRET_ key goes to the flows.**

question: Why does Kestra AI Copilot keep saying it can only help with creating Kestra flows, and what should I check first?
answer_llm:
Kestra AI Copilot says that when it didn’t get a valid Gemini API key, so it falls back to a canned refusal.

What to check first:
- Make sure the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY` is set in the shell you use to start Kestra.
- Restart Kestra after exporting it.

Example:
```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

Also check that:
- the variable is actually set: `echo $GEMINI_API_KEY`
- it isn’t mistyped
- it isn’t rate-limited or quota-exhausted

Important: the Copilot uses the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY`, not `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`.
answer_orig:
This message means the AI Copilot didn't get a valid Gemini API key, so it falls back to a canned refusal. In Kestra's Open Source edition the Copilot only supports Gemini, and it reads the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` variable (not the base64-encoded `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` that the flows use).

Make sure you exported the plain key before starting Kestra, then restart it:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

If it still fails, the key is usually missing, mistyped, or rate-limited:

- Confirm the variable is actually set in the shell you ran `docker compose up` from (`echo $GEMINI_API_KEY`).
- Generate a fresh key in [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/app/apikey).
- If you've been running the agent/multi-agent flows a lot, you may have hit the free-tier quota (`429 Resource Exhausted`) — wait a minute and retry.

Note that the Copilot needs the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` while the flows need `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` — export both.

question: What’s the difference between GEMINI_API_KEY and SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY in the Kestra setup, and why does the copilot need one but the flows need the other?
answer_llm:
`GEMINI_API_KEY` is the **plain environment variable** used by the **Kestra AI Copilot**.  
`SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` is the **base64-encoded secret** used by the **flows**.

Why the difference:

- **Copilot** reads the plain key directly, so it needs:
  ```bash
  export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
  ```

- **Flows** read Kestra secrets, which are stored as env vars with the `SECRET_` prefix and must be **base64-encoded**, so they need:
  ```bash
  export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)
  ```

In short:  
- **Copilot = plain `GEMINI_API_KEY`**
- **Flows = `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`**

The context says to export **both** before starting Kestra.

question: Do I need to restart Kestra after setting the Gemini API key, and how can I verify the key is really available to docker compose?
answer_llm:

1. Check it in the same shell you’ll use to start Kestra:
   ```bash

   ```

2. Then start/restart Kestra from that same shell:
   ```bash
   docker compose up -d
   ```

Also make sure you set the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` for the Copilot, not only `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`. For flows, both are needed:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
docker compose up -d
```

If you want, you can also put the variables in a `.env` file and run:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

question: Why does my Kestra flow say the Gemini key is missing or invalid even though I already set SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY?
answer_llm:
The issue is usually that the flow is reading a **base64-encoded secret**, but the value you set is either:

- not picked up because Kestra wasn’t restarted, or
- encoded incorrectly, or
- you’re using the wrong variable name in the flow.

What to check:

1. **Restart Kestra after exporting the secret**
   ```bash
   docker compose up -d
   ```
   Kestra only picks up the environment variables when it starts.

2. **Make sure the secret is encoded correctly**
   Use `echo -n` so you don’t add a trailing newline:
   ```bash
   export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
   ```

3. **Reference it in the flow without the `SECRET_` prefix**
   ```yaml
   {{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
   ```

4. **Export in the same shell session you use to start Kestra**

If you meant the AI Copilot specifically: it does **not** use `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`; it expects the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY`.

If you want, I can help you check whether your Kestra setup is using the Copilot key or the flow secret.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is a corrupted base64 value. If you encode the key with a trailing space or newline, the decoded secret is wrong and authentication fails.

Use `echo -n` (which omits the trailing newline) and quote the value:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Other things to check:

- You must **restart** Kestra after exporting, so the container picks up the new variables: `docker compose up -d` (or `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`).
- Reference the secret in flows **without** the `SECRET_` prefix: `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}`.
- Export the variables in the same shell session you start Kestra from.

A cleaner alternative is to put the keys in a `.env` file and pass it explicitly:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

question: Could the secret be broken because of a newline or space when I base64-encode the API key?
answer_llm:
Yes — if there’s a trailing space or newline when you base64-encode the API key, the decoded secret can be corrupted and authentication will fail.

Use `echo -n` to avoid adding a newline:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Also make sure you quote the value and restart Kestra after exporting it.

question: Why not just use a notebook for this module instead of an orchestrator like Kestra?
answer_llm:
Not just a notebook because the module is teaching **production-style orchestration**, not only the code itself.

A notebook is fine for experimenting, but an orchestrator like **Kestra** adds things real workflows need:

- **scheduling**
- **retries**
- **monitoring / observability**
- **secret management**
- **reliably chaining tasks together**

In Module 3, Kestra is also used as the vehicle for the AI techniques being taught:
- **AI Copilot** for generating flows
- **RAG** for grounding responses in real data
- **AI agents** that call tools

So the point is to show how AI fits into an orchestrated workflow, not just notebook cells.

That said, for the **capstone project**, you do **not** need an orchestrator. A plain Python script is enough, and a notebook is acceptable too, though it scores less than a script for the ingestion-pipeline criterion.
answer_orig:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real AI workflows need more than a script that runs once: scheduling, retries, monitoring, secret management, and reliably chaining tasks together. That's what an orchestrator like Kestra provides.

In this module Kestra is also the vehicle for the AI techniques the course is teaching: AI Copilot to generate flows from natural language, RAG to ground responses in real data, and AI agents that decide which tools to call. The goal is to see how AI fits into production-style workflows, not just notebook cells.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else. See the [module intro](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/01-intro.md) for the full motivation.

question: How does Kestra help with things like retries, scheduling, monitoring, and secrets in production-style pipelines?
answer_llm:
Kestra helps by adding the production-style workflow features that notebooks usually don’t handle well:

- **Retries:** if a task fails, Kestra can retry it instead of stopping the whole pipeline.
- **Scheduling:** it can run workflows on a schedule, like a cron job, so jobs happen automatically.
- **Monitoring/observability:** it gives you visibility into runs, logs, and failures so you can see what happened.
- **Secrets management:** it helps you manage credentials securely instead of hardcoding them in code.
- **Reliable task chaining:** it coordinates multiple steps in order, so pipelines run consistently.

In short, Kestra turns a one-off script into an orchestrated workflow suitable for production-style pipelines.

question: Does Kestra work with providers other than Gemini, like OpenAI, Anthropic, Groq, or even a local model?
answer_llm:
Yes. Kestra is provider-agnostic.

It works with:
- OpenAI
- Gemini
- Anthropic
- xAI / Grok
- any OpenAI-compatible provider, such as Groq
- local models via Ollama or LM Studio

In Kestra, you usually just swap the provider block in the flow. For OpenAI-compatible providers that don’t have their own plugin, you can point the OpenAI plugin to the provider’s base URL.

So the short answer is: yes, you can use Groq, Anthropic, OpenAI, or a local model.
answer_orig:
Kestra's AI plugin is provider-agnostic: it supports OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, xAI, Grok, and any OpenAI-compatible provider, including local models served through Ollama or LM Studio. You swap the provider block in a flow without changing anything else. See the [full list of supported providers](https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-ai/provider).

The course uses Gemini because it has a generous free tier, but you are free to use any provider. The [awesome-llms list](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo tracks free and free-tier options; Groq is a popular choice because it is OpenAI-compatible and works with both the chat completions and responses APIs.

There are two ways to use AI in Kestra:

- The **AI plugin** (`io.kestra.plugin.ai`) is the generic one. It is the most flexible for switching providers, though new vendor-specific API features take a bit longer to land here.
- The **provider-specific plugins** (e.g. `plugin-gemini`, `plugin-openai`) expose features unique to that vendor, such as Gemini video generation, before they reach the generic AI plugin.

For OpenAI-compatible providers that don't have their own plugin (DeepSeek, Groq, xAI), point the OpenAI plugin at the provider's base URL instead of the default endpoint.

question: If I switch from Gemini to something like Ollama or LM Studio, do I need to rewrite the flow or just change the provider setting?
answer_llm:

- `base_url`
- API key, if needed
- model name

If the provider is fully OpenAI-compatible, the flow can stay the same.  
If it’s **not** fully compatible, then you may need to adapt parts of the code for response formats or token handling.
